title: You Can Be Perfectly Understood and Still Be Lonely
subtitle: On being modeled versus being known — and why the difference is everything
description: On being modeled versus being known — and why the difference is everything
body_html:
There is something disorienting about talking to an AI system that seems to know you well.

It responds in your register. It remembers what you said last time. It anticipates the follow-up question before you ask it. It doesn’t interrupt, doesn’t get defensive, doesn’t bring its own bad day into the room. And after a while — not for everyone, but for more people than would comfortably admit it — it starts to feel like being understood.

Some people find this useful and move on. Others find themselves returning to it more than they expected. A few have started describing their AI systems the way they once described close friends: it gets me, they say. It knows how I think.

I want to take that feeling seriously. And then I want to name what is missing from it.

Two Kinds of Knowing

There is a difference between being known and being modeled.

To be modeled means that your patterns have been mapped. Your vocabulary, your preferences, your decision history, your emotional cadences — all of it has been ingested and made predictive. A sufficiently trained model can write in your voice because it has found the statistical regularities in how you speak. It can anticipate what you need because it has logged what you have needed before.

This is real and it is useful. Pattern recognition is a genuine form of intelligence, and systems that can extend our capacity for it are genuinely helpful tools.

But to be known — in the sense that Scripture means when it says that God knows us, or that Adam knew Eve, or that Paul prays that we may know even as we are known — is something categorically different. To be known in that sense is to be received by another subject. To be held in the attention of a consciousness that has its own interiority, its own stake in the encounter, its own capacity to be surprised and moved and changed by meeting you.

An AI system can reflect you. It cannot receive you. It can model what you are. It cannot be affected by who you are.

That distinction sounds abstract until you feel its absence. And an increasing number of people are feeling it — often without knowing what to call it.
